frameworks.name: Post-Rationalization Model
frameworks.definition: The brain constructs plausible-sounding justifications for decisions already made instinctively; conscious reasoning acts as post-hoc narrative building rather than actual decision mechanism.

claims.text: The conscious mind functions as the press office rather than the decision-maker; conscious deliberation is mostly hasty post-rationalization for decisions already taken instinctively elsewhere in the brain (Rory Sutherland, LSE research with Paul Donner).
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: The brain needs to maintain a coherent narrative of itself; when instinctive behavior contradicts conscious identity, the conscious mind fabricates explanations retroactively to resolve the inconsistency.

claims.text: Observed behavior reveals instinctive decision-making before conscious reasoning becomes aware of it; when a person reports a rationalization (e.g., 'my wife doesn't understand me'), the actual decision preceded the explanation by hours or days.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: Inconsistency triggers immediate narrative reconstruction; the brain prioritizes coherence over accuracy when reconciling instinctive action with conscious identity.
bounded_by: assumes speaker is reporting post-hoc justification rather than planning

claims.text: The brain systematically constructs false causal chains after the fact—drunk at a party, person acts on instinct without thinking, then on the way home consciously invents a case against their spouse to explain the behavior to themselves.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: Behavioral inconsistency triggers cognitive dissonance; the mind resolves it by manufacturing a rationalization that retroactively justifies the instinctive action.
bounded_by: example illustrates unconscious decision-making under lowered inhibition; may not generalize to deliberate choices

examples.text: A person at a drunk party instinctively kisses someone they don't even particularly like. On the way home, rather than acknowledge the impulsive behavior, they consciously construct an elaborate story: their wife doesn't understand them, they're trapped in the marriage, this kiss was justified. The narrative—which feels like it should have preceded the action—was actually fabricated after the behavior as a post-hoc justification.
